agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This architecture exhibits several critical anti-patterns that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit scalability. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-saturated connection pools creates a shared fate failure domain. The 4-deep synchronous call chain amplifies latency and creates tight temporal coupling. Shared Redis for auth, shared NFS for logs, and a monolithic deployment pipeline further erode the independence that microservices are supposed to provide. In its current form, this is a distributed monolith — paying the complexity tax of microservices while gaining none of the benefits.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway","responsibility":"Entry point, request routing, possibly auth enforcement","boundaries":"Should be the only externally-exposed surface; currently couples downstream via synchronous chains"},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management","boundaries":"Initiates synchronous chain to Courier Service; tightly coupled to downstream availability"},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment and dispatch","boundaries":"Depends synchronously on external Carrier API — introduces unbounded latency"},{"name":"Carrier API (External)","responsibility":"Third-party carrier integration","boundaries":"External system outside team's control; latency and availability are unpredictable"},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sending notifications to users","boundaries":"Sits at the tail of a 4-hop synchronous chain; failures here block the entire order flow"},{"name":"Remaining 4 Services","responsibility":"Unknown — not specified","boundaries":"Each consumes 12 connections from the shared PostgreSQL pool"},{"name":"PostgreSQL (Single Instance)","responsibility":"Primary data store for all 8 services","boundaries":"Single point of failure; shared schema or shared instance creates coupling between all services"},{"name":"Redis (Shared)","responsibility":"Session/auth token cache for all services","boundaries":"Hit on every request by every service; single point of failure for all authentication"},{"name":"NFS Mount (Shared)","responsibility":"Centralized log storage","boundaries":"Shared I/O surface; NFS failures affect all services simultaneously"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Carrier API","contract":"Synchronous HTTP (external)","coupling_type":"temporal + platform"},{"from":"Carrier API","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"All Services","to":"PostgreSQL","contract":"SQL over TCP (connection pool)","coupling_type":"data + deployment + failure domain"},{"from":"All Services","to":"Redis","contract":"Redis protocol per request","coupling_type":"temporal + failure domain"},{"from":"All Services","to":"NFS Mount","contract":"File I/O","coupling_type":"infrastructure + failure domain"}],"data_flow":"Every user request enters the API Gateway, which synchronously calls downstream services. Each hop validates the session against shared Redis. The longest chain (Gateway → Order → Courier → Carrier → Notification) means a single order request must complete 4 synchronous network hops plus 5 Redis round-trips for auth before returning. All services read/write to the same PostgreSQL instance and append logs to a shared NFS volume.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Shared Database","rationale":"All 8 services use a single PostgreSQL instance — this is an anti-pattern in microservices, not a deliberate choice. It creates the tightest possible data coupling."},{"pattern":"Synchronous Request Chain","rationale":"Used for simplicity but creates multiplicative latency and cascading failure risk."},{"pattern":"Centralized Session Cache","rationale":"Shared Redis for auth simplifies session management but creates a single point of failure and a per-request bottleneck."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","OrderLineItem"]},{"name":"Courier/Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment","Delivery"]},{"name":"Carrier Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierRequest","ShipmentTracking"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationTemplate","NotificationRecord"]},{"name":"Authentication/Session","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Session","UserToken"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier/Delivery","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (currently synchronous — should be async events)"},{"upstream":"Courier/Delivery","downstream":"Carrier Integration","relationship":"Conformist (bound to external Carrier API contract)"},{"upstream":"Carrier Integration","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Currently synchronous chain — should be event-driven with ACL"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single PostgreSQL instance for all services","gains":"Simplicity: one database to manage, back up, and monitor. No distributed transaction complexity.","sacrifices":"All 8 services share a failure domain. Schema changes in one service risk breaking others. Connection pool is at 96% capacity with zero headroom. Cannot scale services independently.","alternatives_considered":["Database-per-service with eventual consistency","Logical separation via schemas within one instance as interim step","Read replicas to distribute read load"]},{"decision":"Synchronous HTTP call chains","gains":"Simple request-response model. Easy to reason about. Distributed tracing is straightforward.","sacrifices":"Latency multiplies across hops. Any service in the chain going down takes down the entire flow. Notification delivery blocks order creation — a fundamentally incorrect coupling.","alternatives_considered":["Async messaging (event bus) for non-critical path operations","Choreography-based saga for order fulfillment","CQRS to separate read and write paths"]},{"decision":"Shared Redis for session validation on every request","gains":"Consistent auth across all services. Simple implementation.","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a single point of failure for the entire platform. Every request adds Redis round-trip latency. No graceful degradation path.","alternatives_considered":["JWT tokens with local validation (no network call needed)","Token introspection with local caching and TTL","Sidecar-based auth (service mesh)"]},{"decision":"Monolithic CI/CD pipeline","gains":"Deployment simplicity. All services guaranteed to be at compatible versions.","sacrifices":"Eliminates independent deployability — the primary operational benefit of microservices. A bug in Notification Service blocks deployment of Order Service. Blast radius of every deployment is the entire system.","alternatives_considered":["Per-service pipelines with contract testing","Canary deployments per service","Feature flags to decouple deployment from release"]},{"decision":"Shared NFS mount for logs","gains":"Simple log aggregation — all logs in one place.","sacrifices":"NFS failure causes all services to block on I/O. Write contention under load. NFS is not designed for high-throughput concurrent append workloads. Cannot scale log ingestion independently.","alternatives_considered":["Structured logging to stdout with centralized log shipper (Fluentd/Vector)","Direct shipping to Elasticsearch/Loki","Async log buffering with local fallback"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Service decomposition is already done","evolution_path":"The 8 services exist as separate codebases — introducing async messaging between them does not require re-decomposition, only changing the communication pattern."},{"area":"Redis can be replaced for auth","evolution_path":"Switching to JWT-based auth with local validation is a service-level change that can be rolled out per-service behind a feature flag."},{"area":"Notification Service decoupling","evolution_path":"Notification is the easiest service to move to async — it is at the tail of the chain and has no return value needed by upstream callers."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Single PostgreSQL instance","reason":"All services are coupled through shared data. Migrating to database-per-service requires schema separation, data migration, and potentially introducing eventual consistency — a multi-quarter effort."},{"area":"Synchronous call chain (Order → Courier → Carrier → Notification)","reason":"Breaking this chain requires introducing a message broker and converting to async patterns, which changes error handling, retry logic, and user-facing response semantics."},{"area":"Monolithic deployment pipeline","reason":"Splitting into per-service pipelines requires contract testing infrastructure, independent versioning strategy, and possibly API gateway routing changes."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Connection pool exhaustion: 96 of 100 connections allocated. Any traffic spike, slow query, or connection leak will starve services of database connections, causing cascading timeouts across all 8 services simultaneously.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: reduce per-service pool to 10 (80 total), reserving 20 for admin/monitoring. Short-term: add PgBouncer as a connection multiplexer. Medium-term: move toward database-per-service or at minimum read replicas."},{"risk":"Cascading failure via synchronous chain: the 4-hop chain (Gateway → Order → Courier → Carrier → Notification) means p99 latency is the SUM of all 4 services' p99 latencies. If Carrier API (external, uncontrolled) is slow, the entire order flow hangs. No circuit breakers mentioned.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Add circuit breakers (Hystrix/Resilience4j) at every synchronous boundary immediately. Decouple Notification Service to async (fire-and-forget via message queue). Add timeouts at every hop with timeout budgets that decrease down the chain."},{"risk":"Redis SPOF for authentication: if Redis goes down, no service can validate sessions. 100% of traffic fails. If Redis is slow, every request is slow (auth check is on the critical path of every request in every service).","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Switch to JWT tokens with asymmetric key validation (no network call needed). If JWT is not feasible short-term, add Redis Sentinel/Cluster for HA plus local auth caching with short TTL (30-60s) as fallback."},{"risk":"NFS mount failure blocks all services: NFS is a shared infrastructure dependency. If the NFS server becomes unavailable or slow, synchronous log writes will cause thread pool exhaustion in all 8 services simultaneously.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Switch to async logging to stdout/stderr. Use a log shipper (Fluentd, Vector, Filebeat) to collect and forward. This eliminates the shared I/O dependency entirely."},{"risk":"Monolithic deployment creates whole-system blast radius: a regression in any single service forces rollback of all 8 services. Deployment frequency is constrained to the slowest-moving service's release cadence.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Split into per-service pipelines. Start with the most independently-deployable services (e.g., Notification). Implement contract testing between services to ensure compatibility."},{"risk":"No horizontal scalability path: the single PostgreSQL instance cannot be horizontally scaled. Adding more service instances only increases pressure on the already-saturated connection pool.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Introduce PgBouncer for connection multiplexing. Add read replicas for read-heavy services. Plan database-per-service migration for services with independent data models."},{"risk":"Hidden temporal coupling in the call chain: Notification Service is synchronously called at the END of order creation. A user's order confirmation is blocked until their notification is sent. This conflates order persistence (critical) with notification delivery (best-effort).","severity":"high","mitigation":"Make notification async immediately. Order Service should emit an OrderCreated event; Notification Service subscribes. Order creation returns success as soon as the order is persisted."},{"risk":"Carrier API (external) has no SLA guarantees: an external API in the middle of a synchronous chain means an external vendor's outage becomes YOUR outage. No bulkhead isolation mentioned.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Add aggressive timeouts (2-3s max), circuit breaker, and fallback behavior (queue for retry). Consider making carrier assignment async — accept the order, assign carrier in background."},{"risk":"Shared database schema coupling: if all 8 services access the same tables or have cross-service foreign keys, any schema migration becomes a coordination nightmare requiring simultaneous deployment of all affected services.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Audit cross-service table access. Introduce schema-per-service within PostgreSQL as a first step toward full separation. Enforce that services only access their own schema."},{"risk":"No observable failure isolation: with shared DB, shared Redis, shared NFS, and shared deployment, there is no way to isolate a failure to a single service. Monitoring and alerting cannot distinguish between service-level and infrastructure-level failures.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Introduce per-service health checks, metrics, and dashboards. Add distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) across the synchronous chain. Implement per-service resource quotas."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Prioritized remediation in 4 phases. Phase 1 (Week 1-2, zero downtime): Add circuit breakers and timeouts to all synchronous calls. Switch logging from NFS to stdout + log shipper. Reduce connection pools to 10/service. Phase 2 (Week 3-4): Replace Redis session validation with JWT tokens using asymmetric key pairs for local validation. Decouple Notification Service to async via a lightweight message queue (SQS, RabbitMQ, or Redis Streams). Phase 3 (Month 2): Deploy PgBouncer in front of PostgreSQL. Split CI/CD pipeline — start with Notification Service as pilot. Introduce per-service schemas in PostgreSQL. Phase 4 (Quarter 2): Evaluate database-per-service for Order and Courier services. Convert Order→Courier communication to event-driven saga. Introduce API versioning.","files_to_create_or_modify":["Circuit breaker configuration for each service's HTTP client","Timeout budget configuration (e.g., Gateway: 10s, Order: 7s, Courier: 4s, Carrier call: 2s)","Logging configuration: switch from file appender to stdout","JWT key generation and validation middleware to replace Redis session checks","Message queue consumer in Notification Service","PgBouncer configuration (pgbouncer.ini)","Per-service CI/CD pipeline definitions","Database migration scripts to introduce per-service schemas"],"patterns_to_follow":"Apply Bulkhead pattern to isolate failure domains. Use Circuit Breaker at every synchronous boundary. Apply Timeout Budget pattern — each layer gets a decreasing timeout. Use Strangler Fig pattern to incrementally migrate from synchronous to asynchronous communication. Apply Database-per-Service as the target state, with Schema-per-Service as the interim step.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do not add more services to the existing PostgreSQL instance without PgBouncer — you are 4 connections from hard failure","Do not add caching to work around database connection limits — this masks the root cause and introduces cache coherence problems","Do not introduce a message broker and keep synchronous calls as fallback — this creates two communication paths and doubles the debugging surface","Do not split the CI/CD pipeline without contract testing in place — you will trade deployment coupling for integration failures","Do not attempt database-per-service migration before establishing schema boundaries — migrating without clear ownership will create data inconsistencies","Do not use Redis Cluster as the fix for the auth SPOF — the correct fix is eliminating the per-request network call entirely via JWT"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Connection pool saturation risk is arithmetic — 96/100 is objectively critical","Synchronous chain failure modes are well-documented in distributed systems literature","NFS as a log sink for microservices is a known anti-pattern","Monolithic deployment negating microservice benefits is definitional"],"low_confidence_areas":["Unknown whether services share tables/schemas or have clean data boundaries — severity of database coupling could be higher or lower","Unknown what the other 4 unspecified services do — they may introduce additional risks","Unknown traffic patterns and load — connection pool risk could be imminent or months away","Unknown whether services have any existing circuit breakers, retries, or timeout configuration"],"assumptions":["All 8 services are roughly equal in traffic and connection usage","No PgBouncer or similar connection multiplexer is in place","No circuit breakers or timeout budgets currently exist","The Carrier API is a third-party external service, not an internal service","Services share a PostgreSQL instance but ideally have or should have separate schemas","The system is in production and serving real traffic"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"This architecture exhibits several critical anti-patterns that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit scalability. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-saturated connection pools creates a shared fate failure domain. The 4-deep synchronous call chain amplifies latency and creates tight temporal coupling. Shared Redis for auth, shared NFS for logs, and a monolithic deployment pipeline further erode the independence that microservices are supposed to

Adding more service instances only increases pressure on the already-saturated connection pool.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Introduce PgBouncer for connection multiplexing. trace.result: {"executive_summary":"This architecture exhibits several critical anti-patterns that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit scalability. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-saturated connection pools creates a shared fate failure domain. The 4-deep synchronous call chain amplifies latency and creates tight temporal coupling. Shared Redis for auth, shared NFS for logs, and a monolithic deployment pipeline further erode the independence that microservices are supposed to provide. In its current form, this is a distributed monolith — paying the complexity tax of microservices while gaining none of the benefits.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway","responsibility":"Entry point, request routing, possibly auth enforcement","boundaries":"Should be the only externally-exposed surface; currently couples downstream via synchronous chains"},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management","boundaries":"Initiates synchronous chain to Courier Service; tightly coupled to downstream availability"},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment and dispatch","boundaries":"Depends synchronously on external Carrier API — introduces unbounded latency"},{"name":"Carrier API (External)","responsibility":"Third-party carrier integration","boundaries":"External system outside team's control; latency and availability are unpredictable"},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sending notifications to users","boundaries":"Sits at the tail of a 4-hop synchronous chain; failures here block the entire order flow"},{"name":"Remaining 4 Services","responsibility":"Unknown — not specified","boundaries":"Each consumes 12 connections from the shared PostgreSQL pool"},{"name":"PostgreSQL (Single Instance)","responsibility":"Primary data store for all 8 services","boundaries":"Single point of failure; Each hop validates the session against shared Redis. The longest chain (Gateway → Order → Courier → Carrier → Notification) means a single order request must complete 4 synchronous network hops plus 5 Redis round-trips for auth before returning. All services read/write to the same PostgreSQL instance and append logs to a shared NFS volume.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Shared Database","rationale":"All 8 services use a single PostgreSQL instance — this is an anti-pattern in microservices, not a deliberate choice. It creates the tightest possible data coupling."},{"pattern":"Synchronous Request Chain","rationale":"Used for simplicity but creates multiplicative latency and cascading failure risk."},{"pattern":"Centralized Session Cache","rationale":"Shared Redis for auth simplifies session management but creates a single point of failure and a per-request bottleneck."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","OrderLineItem"]},{"name":"Courier/Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment","Delivery"]},{"name":"Carrier Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierRequest","ShipmentTracking"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationTemplate","NotificationRecord"]},{"name":"Authentication/Session","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Session","UserToken"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier/Delivery","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (currently synchronous — should be async events)"},{"upstream":"Courier/Delivery","downstream":"Carrier Integration","relationship":"Conformist (bound to external Carrier API contract)"},{"upstream":"Carrier Integration","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Currently synchronous chain — should be event-driven with ACL"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single PostgreSQL instance for all services","gains":"Simplicity: one database to manage, back up, and monitor. No distributed transaction complexity.","sacrifices":"All 8 services share a failure domain. Schema changes in one service risk breaking others. Connection pool is at 96% capacity with zero headroom. Cannot scale services independently.","alternatives_considered":["Database-per-service with eventual consistency","Logical separation via schemas within one instance as interim step","Read replicas to distribute read load"]},{"decision":"Synchronous HTTP call chains","gains":"Simple request-response model. Easy to reason about. Distributed tracing is straightforward.","sacrifices":"Latency multiplies across hops. Any service in the chain going down takes down the entire flow. Notification delivery blocks order creation — a fundamentally incorrect coupling.","alternatives_considered":["Async messaging (event bus) for non-critical path operations","Choreography-based saga for order fulfillment","CQRS to separate read and write paths"]},{"decision":"Shared Redis for session validation on every request","gains":"Consistent auth across all services. Simple implementation.","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a single point of failure for the entire platform. Every request adds Redis round-trip latency. No graceful degradation path.","alternatives_considered":["JWT tokens with local validation (no network call needed)","Token introspection with local caching and TTL","Sidecar-based auth (service mesh)"]},{"decision":"Monolithic CI/CD pipeline","gains":"Deployment simplicity. All services guaranteed to be at compatible versions.","sacrifices":"Eliminates independent deployability — the primary operational benefit of microservices. A bug in Notification Service blocks deployment of Order Service. Blast radius of every deployment is the entire system.","alternatives_considered":["Per-service pipelines with contract testing","Canary deployments per service","Feature flags to decouple deployment from release"]},{"decision":"Shared NFS mount for logs","gains":"Simple log aggregation — all logs in one place.","sacrifices":"NFS failure causes all services to block on I/O. Write contention under load. NFS is not designed for high-throughput concurrent append workloads. Cannot scale log ingestion independently.","alternatives_considered":["Structured logging to stdout with centralized log shipper (Fluentd/Vector)","Direct shipping to Elasticsearch/Loki","Async log buffering with local fallback"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Service decomposition is already done","evolution_path":"The 8 services exist as separate codebases — introducing async messaging between them does not require re-decomposition, only changing the communication pattern."},{"area":"Redis can be replaced for auth","evolution_path":"Switching to JWT-based auth with local validation is a service-level change that can be rolled out per-service behind a feature flag."},{"area":"Notification Service decoupling","evolution_path":"Notification is the easiest service to move to async — it is at the tail of the chain and has no return value needed by upstream callers."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Single PostgreSQL instance","reason":"All services are coupled through shared data. Migrating to database-per-service requires schema separation, data migration, and potentially introducing eventual consistency — a multi-quarter effort."},{"area":"Synchronous call chain (Order → Courier → Carrier → Notification)","reason":"Breaking this chain requires introducing a message broker and converting to async patterns, which changes error handling, retry logic, and user-facing response semantics."},{"area":"Monolithic deployment pipeline","reason":"Splitting into per-service pipelines requires contract testing infrastructure, independent versioning strategy, and possibly API gateway routing changes."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Connection pool exhaustion: 96 of 100 connections allocated. Any traffic spike, slow query, or connection leak will starve services of database connections, causing cascading timeouts across all 8 services simultaneously.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: reduce per-service pool to 10 (80 total), reserving 20 for admin/monitoring. Short-term: add PgBouncer as a connection multiplexer. Medium-term: move toward database-per-service or at minimum read replicas."},{"risk":"Cascading failure via synchronous chain: the 4-hop chain (Gateway → Order → Courier → Carrier → Notification) means p99 latency is the SUM of all 4 services' p99 latencies. If Carrier API (external, uncontrolled) is slow, the entire order flow hangs. No circuit breakers mentioned.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Add circuit breakers (Hystrix/Resilience4j) at every synchronous boundary immediately. Decouple Notification Service to async (fire-and-forget via message queue). Add timeouts at every hop with timeout budgets that decrease down the chain."},{"risk":"Redis SPOF for authentication: if Redis goes down, no service can validate sessions. 100% of traffic fails. If Redis is slow, every request is slow (auth check is on the critical path of every request in every service).","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Switch to JWT tokens with asymmetric key validation (no network call needed). If JWT is not feasible short-term, add Redis Sentinel/Cluster for HA plus local auth caching with short TTL (30-60s) as fallback."},{"risk":"NFS mount failure blocks all services: NFS is a shared infrastructure dependency. If the NFS server becomes unavailable or slow, synchronous log writes will cause thread pool exhaustion in all 8 services simultaneously.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Switch to async logging to stdout/stderr. Use a log shipper (Fluentd, Vector, Filebeat) to collect and forward. This eliminates the shared I/O dependency entirely."},{"risk":"Monolithic deployment creates whole-system blast radius: a regression in any single service forces rollback of all 8 services. Deployment frequency is constrained to the slowest-moving service's release cadence.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Split into per-service pipelines. Start with the most independently-deployable services (e.g., Notification). Implement contract testing between services to ensure compatibility."},{"risk":"No horizontal scalability path: the single PostgreSQL instance cannot be horizontally scaled. Adding more service instances only increases pressure on the already-saturated connection pool.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Introduce PgBouncer for connection multiplexing. Add read replicas for read-heavy services. Plan database-per-service migration for services with independent data models."},{"risk":"Hidden temporal coupling in the call chain: Notification Service is synchronously called at the END of order creation. A user's order confirmation is blocked until their notification is sent. This conflates order persistence (critical) with notification delivery (best-effort).","severity":"high","mitigation":"Make notification async immediately. Order Service should emit an OrderCreated event; Notification Service subscribes. Order creation returns success as soon as the order is persisted."},{"risk":"Carrier API (external) has no SLA guarantees: an external API in the middle of a synchronous chain means an external vendor's outage becomes YOUR outage. No bulkhead isolation mentioned.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Add aggressive timeouts (2-3s max), circuit breaker, and fallback behavior (queue for retry). Consider making carrier assignment async — accept the order, assign carrier in background."},{"risk":"Shared database schema coupling: if all 8 services access the same tables or have cross-service foreign keys, any schema migration becomes a coordination nightmare requiring simultaneous deployment of all affected services.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Audit cross-service table access. Introduce schema-per-service within PostgreSQL as a first step toward full separation. Enforce that services only access their own schema."},{"risk":"No observable failure isolation: with shared DB, shared Redis, shared NFS, and shared deployment, there is no way to isolate a failure to a single service. Monitoring and alerting cannot distinguish between service-level and infrastructure-level failures.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Introduce per-service health checks, metrics, and dashboards. Add distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) across the synchronous chain. Implement per-service resource quotas."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Prioritized remediation in 4 phases. Phase 1 (Week 1-2, zero downtime): Add circuit breakers and timeouts to all synchronous calls. Switch logging from NFS to stdout + log shipper. Reduce connection pools to 10/service. Phase 2 (Week 3-4): Replace Redis session validation with JWT tokens using asymmetric key pairs for local validation. Decouple Notification Service to async via a lightweight message queue (SQS, RabbitMQ, or Redis Streams). Phase 3 (Month 2): Deploy PgBouncer in front of PostgreSQL. Split CI/CD pipeline — start with Notification Service as pilot. Introduce per-service schemas in PostgreSQL. Phase 4 (Quarter 2): Evaluate database-per-service for Order and Courier services. Convert Order→Courier communication to event-driven saga. Introduce API versioning.","files_to_create_or_modify":["Circuit breaker configuration for each service's HTTP client","Timeout budget configuration (e.g., Gateway: 10s, Order: 7s, Courier: 4s, Carrier call: 2s)","Logging configuration: switch from file appender to stdout","JWT key generation and validation middleware to replace Redis session checks","Message queue consumer in Notification Service","PgBouncer configuration (pgbouncer.ini)","Per-service CI/CD pipeline definitions","Database migration scripts to introduce per-service schemas"],"patterns_to_follow":"Apply Bulkhead pattern to isolate failure domains. Use Circuit Breaker at every synchronous boundary. Apply Timeout Budget pattern — each layer gets a decreasing timeout. Use Strangler Fig pattern to incrementally migrate from synchronous to asynchronous communication.
